lessons.title: What an Oscillator Is
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Open a laptop, a watch, or a microwave and somewhere inside, a tiny circuit is quietly making a signal go up and down, over and over, billions of times a second. That circuit is an **oscillator**: a circuit that produces a repeating signal all by itself, with no input signal feeding it. You give it steady DC power (like from a battery), and it gives back a wave that keeps wiggling on its own.

**Build.** Compare it to an amplifier. An amplifier needs something to amplify: a signal goes in, a bigger copy comes out. An oscillator needs nothing going in. It is more like a metronome that, once wound up, keeps ticking on its own rhythm.

The repeating signal is called a **waveform**, and its shape has a name:
- A **sine wave** is the smooth, rounded up-and-down shape: a pure musical tone.
- A **square wave** snaps between a high level and a low level, like a light switching fully on and fully off: this is what a digital **clock** signal looks like.
- A **triangle wave** ramps straight up, then straight down.

Two words describe any waveform. Its **frequency** is how many full repeats happen per second, measured in **hertz (Hz)**; 1000 Hz means a thousand repeats every second. Its **amplitude** is how big the wiggle is (how far up and down it swings).

Everyday oscillators are everywhere: the **crystal** that beats out time inside every computer and digital watch, the tone generator that makes a phone beep, a metronome for music, and the carrier wave a radio station transmits.

**Recap.** An oscillator turns steady DC power into a repeating waveform, with no input. The shape can be a sine, square, or triangle; frequency is repeats per second, amplitude is how big the swing is.

items.prompt: Study the diagram of common waveform shapes. What is the single most defining feature of an oscillator?
choices: It makes an input signal larger
It produces a repeating signal by itself, with no input signal
It converts a repeating signal into steady DC
It blocks all signals above a cutoff frequency
items.explanation: An oscillator is self-starting: fed only steady DC power, it produces a repeating waveform with no input signal. Making an input larger describes an amplifier, not an oscillator. Converting AC to DC is a rectifier, and blocking frequencies is a filter.

items.prompt: How does an oscillator differ from an amplifier?
choices: An amplifier needs an input signal to enlarge; an oscillator needs no input signal
An oscillator needs an input signal; an amplifier does not
They are exactly the same circuit
An amplifier only works with DC, an oscillator only with AC
items.explanation: An amplifier takes a signal in and puts a bigger copy out, so it always needs an input. An oscillator makes its own repeating signal from steady DC power with no input. The reversed option describes them backwards.

items.prompt: What does the frequency of a waveform measure?
choices: How big the up-and-down swing is
How many full repeats happen per second
The DC supply voltage
The shape of the wave
items.explanation: Frequency counts how many complete cycles occur each second, measured in hertz (Hz). How big the swing is describes amplitude, not frequency. The shape (sine, square, triangle) and the supply voltage are separate properties.

items.prompt: A waveform repeats 1000 times every second. Its frequency is:
choices: 1000 hertz (1 kHz)
1000 volts
1 hertz
1000 seconds
items.explanation: One repeat per second is 1 hertz, so 1000 repeats per second is 1000 Hz, also written 1 kHz. Volts measure voltage, not how often a wave repeats, and seconds are a unit of time, not frequency.

items.prompt: Which waveform snaps between a high level and a low level, like a switch turning fully on and off, and is the typical shape of a digital clock signal?
choices: Sine wave
Triangle wave
Square wave
Flat DC line
items.explanation: A square wave jumps between two levels, matching the on/off nature of a digital clock. A sine wave is smoothly rounded, a triangle wave ramps up and down in straight lines, and a flat DC line does not repeat at all.

items.prompt: What does the amplitude of a waveform describe?
choices: How many times it repeats each second
How big its up-and-down swing is
Its color
Whether it is a sine or a square
items.explanation: Amplitude is the size of the swing, how far the signal moves up and down. How often it repeats is frequency, and whether it is a sine or square is the shape. Signals do not have a color.

items.prompt: Which of these is a real, everyday example of an oscillator?
choices: The crystal that keeps time inside a digital watch
A resistor that limits current to an LED
A battery that stores energy
A switch that turns a lamp on
items.explanation: The timekeeping crystal in a watch is an oscillator: it produces a steady repeating signal used to count time. A resistor, a battery, and a plain switch do not generate a repeating waveform on their own.

items.prompt: An oscillator is powered only by a steady DC supply (no signal input). What comes out?
choices: Nothing, because there is no input signal
A steady DC voltage identical to the supply
A repeating (AC) waveform such as a sine or square wave
A single brief pulse, then nothing
items.explanation: That is the whole point of an oscillator: steady DC in, a continuous repeating waveform out. It does not need an input signal, it does not just pass the DC through unchanged, and it keeps repeating rather than producing one lone pulse.

items.prompt: Which everyday comparison best captures how an oscillator behaves?
choices: A funnel that pours water through faster
A wound-up metronome that keeps ticking on its own rhythm
A mirror that reflects whatever you show it
A bucket that fills up once and stops
items.explanation: A metronome, once running, produces a steady repeating beat on its own, just like an oscillator producing a repeating waveform. A funnel and a mirror only act on something fed to them, and a bucket that fills once does not repeat.

items.prompt: Two oscillators make sine waves. One repeats 100 times per second, the other 100,000 times per second. Which statement is correct?
choices: The first has the higher frequency
The second has the higher frequency
They have the same frequency because both are sine waves
Frequency depends only on the shape, so it cannot be compared
items.explanation: Frequency is repeats per second, so 100,000 per second (100 kHz) is far higher than 100 per second (100 Hz). The shape being a sine wave in both cases does not make their frequencies equal; frequency and shape are independent.

lessons.title: Positive Feedback & the Barkhausen Idea
lessons.content:
**Hook.** How does a circuit keep a wave going forever, all by itself? The trick is **feedback**: the circuit listens to its own output and feeds part of it back to its own input. Done right, the signal keeps refreshing itself, like a singer holding a microphone too close to a speaker and getting that growing howl.

**Build.** You may have met **negative feedback** with op-amps: there, the fed-back signal *opposes* the input and calms the circuit down, keeping it stable. An oscillator does the opposite. It uses **positive feedback**: the fed-back signal arrives *in step* with the input and *reinforces* it. Each trip around the loop adds to the signal instead of cancelling it, so a tiny wiggle builds into a full, sustained wave.

Think of the loop as two parts: an **amplifier** with a gain (call it $A$, how much bigger it makes a signal) and a **feedback network** that sends a fraction (call it $\beta$) of the output back to the input. The product $A\beta$ is the **loop gain**: how much a signal is multiplied after one full trip around the loop.

For a wave to sustain itself, two things must be true at the same frequency. This pair is the **Barkhausen condition**:
1. **Loop gain equals 1.** A signal comes back exactly as strong as it left ($A\beta = 1$). If it came back weaker, it would fade out; stronger, it would keep growing.
2. **Phase equals 0 (or a full 360 degrees).** The fed-back signal lines up perfectly in step with the original. "Phase" just means timing alignment; 0 degrees means perfectly in step, so it reinforces.

The feedback network is also fussy about *which* frequency it lets come back in step. That is how the oscillator picks its one output frequency: only at that frequency does the timing line up.

**Recap.** Positive feedback feeds the output back in step with the input so the signal sustains itself. The Barkhausen condition says oscillation happens when the loop gain is 1 and the phase is 0 (in step), at one frequency.

items.prompt: Study the feedback-loop diagram. What is the role of feedback in an oscillator?
choices: It throws the output away to keep the circuit cool
It sends part of the output back to the input to keep the signal going
It blocks the output from ever reaching the input
It converts the output to DC
items.explanation: Feedback routes part of the output back to the input. With the right timing, this refreshes the signal each cycle so the wave sustains itself. Blocking or discarding the output would stop oscillation, and feedback does not rectify the signal to DC.

items.prompt: An oscillator uses positive feedback. What does "positive" mean here?
choices: The fed-back signal opposes and cancels the input
The fed-back signal arrives in step and reinforces the input
The feedback uses only positive voltages
The circuit only works on a positive power supply
items.explanation: Positive (regenerative) feedback returns the signal in step with the input so it adds to it, letting a small wiggle build into a full wave. A signal that opposes and cancels is negative feedback. "Positive" here is about timing/reinforcement, not about voltage signs or supply polarity.

items.prompt: How does positive feedback (in an oscillator) differ from the negative feedback you saw with op-amps?
choices: Negative feedback reinforces the signal; positive feedback cancels it
Positive feedback reinforces and sustains the signal; negative feedback opposes it and adds stability
They are the same; only the name differs
Positive feedback only works with DC signals
items.explanation: Negative feedback subtracts, calming a circuit and making amplifiers stable. Positive feedback adds, reinforcing the signal so it can sustain oscillation. The first option has them reversed, and positive feedback is used precisely for repeating AC waveforms.

items.prompt: The "loop gain" $A\beta$ is best described as:
choices: The supply voltage divided by the output voltage
How much a signal is multiplied after one full trip around the loop
The number of cycles per second
The resistance of the feedback network
items.explanation: Loop gain is the amplifier gain A times the feedback fraction beta, which is the overall multiplication a signal sees on one complete trip around the loop. It is not a voltage ratio of the supply, not a frequency, and not a resistance.

items.prompt: The Barkhausen condition for steady oscillation requires the loop gain to be:
choices: Much greater than 1 forever
Exactly 1 (the signal returns just as strong as it left)
Exactly 0
Negative
items.explanation: For a steady wave, the signal must return exactly as strong as it left, so loop gain = 1. If it stayed well above 1 the wave would keep growing without limit; at 0 there would be no signal; a negative value alone does not describe sustained oscillation.

items.prompt: The second part of the Barkhausen condition is about phase. What must the phase around the loop be?
choices: 180 degrees, so the signal arrives upside-down
0 degrees (or a full 360), so the fed-back signal arrives in step
90 degrees
Any value at all; phase does not matter
items.explanation: The fed-back signal must arrive in step with the original (0 degrees, equivalently 360) so it reinforces rather than cancels. A 180-degree shift would invert and cancel the signal, and phase very much matters: it is what selects the oscillation frequency.

items.prompt: An oscillator can produce many possible frequencies in principle. What actually pins down the one frequency it settles on?
choices: The supply voltage
The frequency where the feedback timing lines up in step (0-degree phase)
The color of the wires
The amplifier gain alone, regardless of phase
items.explanation: Oscillation can only occur at the single frequency where the loop phase is 0 degrees, so the fed-back signal arrives in step. The frequency-selective feedback network sets where that happens. Supply voltage and gain affect amplitude and startup, not which frequency the timing aligns at.

items.prompt: The howl you hear when a microphone gets too close to its own loudspeaker is a real-world example of:
choices: Negative feedback calming a system
Positive feedback building a signal until it sustains a loud tone
A signal being filtered out
DC power being stored
items.explanation: The mic picks up the speaker, amplifies it, plays it louder, picks it up again: positive feedback in step, building the signal into a sustained howl. That is oscillation. It is the opposite of negative feedback's calming effect, and nothing here is being filtered out or stored as DC.

items.prompt: Both Barkhausen conditions (gain = 1 and phase = 0) must be satisfied:
choices: At completely different frequencies
At the same single frequency, at the same time
Only after the circuit is switched off
Only in negative-feedback circuits
items.explanation: Both conditions have to hold together at one frequency for that frequency to sustain itself. If the gain and phase conditions were met at different frequencies, neither frequency would actually sustain. Barkhausen describes oscillators (positive feedback), not the off state.

items.prompt: If the fed-back signal consistently arrives weaker than it left (loop gain less than 1), what happens to the wave?
choices: It grows without limit
It fades away and dies out
It stays at a perfect constant amplitude
It changes shape into a square wave
items.explanation: If each trip around the loop returns less than it sent, the signal shrinks a little every cycle and eventually dies out. Growing without limit needs loop gain above 1, and a perfectly steady wave needs loop gain of exactly 1. Loop gain does not by itself change the waveform shape.

lessons.title: Why a Real Oscillator Needs a Limiter
lessons.content:
**Hook.** There is a chicken-and-egg puzzle in the Barkhausen idea. To sustain a wave, the loop gain should be exactly 1. But if it is exactly 1 from the start, how does the wave ever get going? A loop gain of 1 only keeps an existing wave the same size; it cannot build one out of nothing.

**Build.** The answer: design the oscillator to start with loop gain a little *greater than 1*. Every real circuit always has faint, random electrical **noise** in it, a tiny natural hiss. With loop gain above 1, that whisper of noise at the right frequency gets multiplied bigger on each trip around the loop, growing and growing, the way the microphone howl swells. That is how an oscillator **starts up** from nothing.

But growth that never stops is a problem. If the wave keeps getting bigger, it eventually hits the limits of the power supply, the **rails**, and the tops and bottoms get chopped flat, turning a clean sine into a distorted, clipped mess.

So a real oscillator needs something to *automatically reduce the gain as the signal gets big*. This is **amplitude limiting** (sometimes called automatic gain control, AGC). As the wave grows, the limiter gently lowers the effective gain until the loop gain drifts down to exactly 1, and the amplitude holds steady at a clean, chosen level. The wave parks itself there.

What does the limiting? Several tricks: a small **incandescent lamp** whose resistance rises as it warms (used in the famous HP200A oscillator), a **diode** clamp, a **JFET** acting as a voltage-controlled resistor, or simply the amplifier softly running out of room. All do the same job: more amplitude leads to less gain.

**Recap.** Start with loop gain above 1 so noise grows the wave into existence; then a limiter (lamp, diode, JFET, or soft saturation) trims the gain back to exactly 1 so the amplitude settles cleanly instead of clipping on the rails.

items.prompt: Study the startup-and-limiting diagram. Why is the loop gain made slightly greater than 1 at startup, instead of exactly 1?
choices: So the oscillator runs cooler
So tiny circuit noise gets multiplied and grows into a full wave
So the frequency is more accurate
Because a loop gain of exactly 1 is impossible to build
items.explanation: With loop gain above 1, faint random noise at the right frequency is amplified more each trip around the loop, building up into a real wave. A loop gain of exactly 1 only holds an existing wave steady, so it could never start one from noise.

items.prompt: Where does the very first signal in an oscillator come from, before any wave exists?
choices: From an input signal supplied by the user
From faint random electrical noise always present in the circuit
From the feedback network generating it on command
From the output of a separate oscillator
items.explanation: Every real circuit has a tiny natural hiss of random noise. The oscillator amplifies the part of that noise at its target frequency until a full wave builds. There is no user input signal, and the feedback network only routes signals, it does not create them from nothing.

items.prompt: If the loop gain stays well above 1 and nothing limits the amplitude, what happens to the output?
choices: It settles at a small, clean level
It grows until it hits the supply rails and clips into a distorted shape
It disappears completely
It stays a perfect sine wave forever
items.explanation: With loop gain stuck above 1, the wave keeps growing until it slams into the power-supply limits (the rails), flattening the peaks and distorting the wave. It cannot stay a clean sine without something to pull the gain back to 1.

items.prompt: What does "amplitude limiting" (AGC) do in an oscillator?
choices: Increases the gain as the signal grows
Reduces the effective gain as the signal grows, settling the loop gain at 1
Sets the oscillation frequency
Converts the sine wave into a square wave
items.explanation: Amplitude limiting lowers the effective gain as the wave gets bigger, easing the loop gain down to exactly 1 so the amplitude holds steady at a clean level. Increasing gain as the signal grows would make clipping worse, and limiting controls size, not frequency or shape.

items.prompt: What does the loop gain settle to once the amplitude has stabilized?
choices: Exactly 1
Exactly 0
As high as possible
Exactly 2
items.explanation: Once the wave reaches its steady amplitude, the limiter has trimmed the effective gain so the loop gain sits at exactly 1, holding the wave the same size each cycle. A loop gain of 0 would kill the wave, and anything above 1 would keep it growing.

items.prompt: The classic HP200A oscillator used a small incandescent lamp for amplitude limiting. How does that work?
choices: The lamp lights up to show the user the frequency
As the signal grows, the lamp warms and its resistance rises, lowering the gain
The lamp stores charge like a capacitor
The lamp blocks DC while passing AC
items.explanation: A bigger signal pushes more current through the lamp's filament, heating it so its resistance increases, which lowers the amplifier's gain toward the point where loop gain equals 1. The lamp is acting as a gentle automatic gain control, not as an indicator, capacitor, or DC block.

items.prompt: The shared idea behind a limiting lamp, a diode clamp, a JFET, or soft amplifier saturation is:
choices: More amplitude should lead to more gain
More amplitude should lead to less gain
They each set the oscillation frequency
They each convert AC to DC
items.explanation: Every amplitude-limiting trick reduces the gain as the signal gets bigger, pulling the loop gain back to 1 so the amplitude settles. Increasing gain with amplitude would cause runaway clipping, and these elements control size, not frequency or rectification.

items.prompt: Why is a loop gain of exactly 1 from the very first moment a bad design for startup?
choices: It would make the frequency drift
It only holds an existing wave steady, so a wave can never build out of noise
It would overheat the circuit
It forces the output into a square wave
items.explanation: Loop gain of 1 keeps a wave the same size each cycle, so with no wave to begin with, nothing ever grows. You need loop gain above 1 at startup so noise can build up. It is a startup problem, not an overheating or waveform-shape issue.

items.prompt: Put the life of a well-designed oscillator in order from switch-on:
choices: Amplitude steady, then noise grows, then gain rises
Noise grows with loop gain above 1, then the limiter trims gain to 1 and amplitude settles
Amplitude clips immediately, then noise appears
Gain stays exactly 1 the whole time and nothing changes
items.explanation: At switch-on, loop gain above 1 lets noise grow into a wave; as the wave gets large, the limiter reduces the gain to exactly 1 and the amplitude holds steady. The other sequences reverse cause and effect or describe a circuit that could never start.

items.prompt: An oscillator's output is a sine wave with its top and bottom peaks flattened off. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The loop gain is too low
The amplitude is growing into the supply rails because limiting is too weak
The phase condition is not met
The supply voltage is too low for any signal
items.explanation: Flattened peaks are clipping: the wave has grown until it hits the power-supply rails, which happens when amplitude limiting is too weak to hold the gain at 1. Too-low loop gain would make the wave fade out, not clip, and a missing phase condition would stop oscillation entirely.

lessons.title: RC Oscillators: Wien-Bridge & Phase-Shift
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Suppose you want to make a clean audio tone, maybe the test beep in a piece of gear. At these everyday low frequencies you can build the frequency-picking part of the loop out of just **resistors (R)** and **capacitors (C)**, the two cheapest, smallest parts in electronics. These are called **RC oscillators**, and two designs do most of the work.

**Build.** Recall that the feedback network's job is to let exactly one frequency come back in step (0-degree phase). A network built from R and C does this because a capacitor's effect changes with frequency, which shifts the timing differently at different frequencies. Only at one special frequency does the timing line up.

**Wien-bridge oscillator.** This pairs an op-amp with a feedback network of one series RC and one parallel RC. At its special frequency
$$f_0 = \\frac{1}{2\\pi RC},$$
the network passes the signal back in step (0-degree phase) but shrinks it to one-third its size. To satisfy "loop gain = 1," the amplifier must make up that loss with a gain of exactly **3**. The Wien-bridge is loved for very *clean*, low-distortion sine waves, especially with a gentle limiter like the warming lamp from the last lesson.

**Phase-shift oscillator.** This uses an amplifier that flips the signal upside-down (a 180-degree shift) plus a ladder of three RC sections that add up to the other 180 degrees at one frequency, so the round trip totals the in-step 360. For three equal sections
$$f_0 = \\frac{1}{2\\pi RC\\sqrt{6}},$$
and the ladder shrinks the signal to one twenty-ninth, so the amplifier needs a gain of about **29**. It is simpler to build but its tone is less pure and its frequency drifts more than the Wien-bridge.

A caution for both: the frequency is only as accurate as your R and C values, which drift with temperature and age. That is exactly why truly precise clocks use crystals instead (a later lesson).

**Recap.** RC oscillators set frequency with resistors and capacitors. The Wien-bridge runs at $f_0 = 1/(2\\pi RC)$ and needs a gain of 3; the phase-shift type needs a gain of about 29. Both are simple but only as stable as their R and C parts.

items.prompt: Study the Wien-bridge diagram. Why are RC (resistor-capacitor) oscillators the natural choice for low, audio frequencies rather than ones using inductors?
choices: Resistors and capacitors cannot work at high frequencies
Inductors for low frequencies are bulky, lossy, and costly, while R and C stay small and cheap
RC oscillators do not need an amplifier
Inductors cannot store energy
items.explanation: At low frequencies the inductors you would need are physically large, lossy, and expensive, whereas resistors and capacitors stay tiny and cheap. RC oscillators still need an amplifier to make up the network's loss; the issue is purely the practicality of the parts.

items.prompt: What sets the oscillation frequency of a Wien-bridge oscillator?
choices: $f_0 = \\dfrac{1}{2\\pi RC}$, set by the resistor and capacitor values
The supply voltage alone
The amplifier's gain alone
items.explanation: The Wien-bridge frequency is f0 = 1/(2-pi-R-C), set by the equal R and C in its network. The first option inverts the formula. Supply voltage and gain affect startup and amplitude, not which frequency the timing lines up at.

items.prompt: At its operating frequency the Wien-bridge network shrinks the signal to one-third. What amplifier gain is therefore needed, and why?
choices: A gain of 3, to make up the 1/3 loss so the loop gain becomes 1
A gain of 1/3, to match the loss
A gain of 29, like the phase-shift type
Any gain at all; it does not matter
items.explanation: If the network passes back only 1/3 of the signal, the amplifier must multiply by 3 so the round-trip loop gain equals 1. A gain of 1/3 would shrink it further and the wave would die. The factor of 29 belongs to the three-section phase-shift oscillator, not the Wien-bridge.

items.prompt: A Wien-bridge oscillator uses $R = 10\\,\\text{k}\\Omega$ and $C = 16\\,\\text{nF}$. Its frequency $f_0 = 1/(2\\pi RC)$ is approximately:
choices: About 100 Hz
About 1 kHz
About 10 kHz
About 1 MHz
items.explanation: f0 = 1/(2-pi x 10000 x 16e-9), which is roughly 995 Hz, about 1 kHz. The other choices are off by factors of ten or more; plugging the values into the formula gives the kilohertz range.

items.prompt: How does a three-section RC phase-shift oscillator achieve the in-step (360-degree) condition?
choices: The amplifier alone provides all 360 degrees
The amplifier flips the signal (180 degrees) and the three RC sections supply the other 180 degrees
It does not need 360 degrees; 90 is enough
The capacitors provide exactly 360 degrees by themselves
items.explanation: An inverting amplifier contributes 180 degrees, and the RC ladder adds the remaining 180 degrees at one particular frequency, totalling the in-step 360. The amplifier cannot do all 360 alone, and oscillation does require the full in-step condition, not 90 degrees.

items.prompt: A three-section RC ladder shrinks the signal to about one twenty-ninth. The amplifier in a phase-shift oscillator therefore needs a gain of about:
items.explanation: To bring the loop gain to 1, the amplifier must make up the ladder's 1/29 loss, so it needs a gain of about 29. A gain of 3 is for the Wien-bridge, and a gain of 1 or 1/29 would not overcome the loss, so the wave would fade.

items.prompt: Compared with the phase-shift oscillator, the Wien-bridge oscillator generally offers:
choices: A less pure tone and worse frequency stability
A purer (lower-distortion) tone and better frequency stability
Identical performance but it needs an inductor
No need for any amplifier
items.explanation: The Wien-bridge, especially with gentle amplitude limiting, gives a cleaner sine wave and steadier frequency than the simpler phase-shift design. The phase-shift type trades that purity and stability for simplicity. Neither uses an inductor, and both need an amplifier.

items.prompt: In both RC oscillators, what is the feedback network essentially doing?
choices: Letting all frequencies pass back equally
Letting one chosen frequency arrive back in step while blocking the timing of others
Converting the sine wave to DC
Amplifying the signal
items.explanation: Because a capacitor's effect changes with frequency, the RC network lines the timing up (0-degree round trip) at just one frequency, which becomes the oscillation frequency. It does not treat all frequencies alike, it does not rectify to DC, and amplification is the separate amplifier's job.

items.prompt: Why is the frequency of an RC oscillator not perfectly stable over time and temperature?
choices: Because resistor and capacitor values drift with temperature and age, and f0 depends on them
Because the supply voltage sets the frequency
Because op-amps cannot oscillate
Because RC oscillators have no feedback
items.explanation: Since f0 = 1/(2-pi-R-C), any drift in R or C with temperature or age shifts the frequency. Supply voltage does not set f0, op-amps do oscillate in these circuits, and RC oscillators absolutely rely on feedback. This drift is why precise clocks use crystals instead.

items.prompt: You need a clean, low-distortion 1 kHz sine tone for testing audio gear. Which is the better fit?
choices: A phase-shift oscillator, because purity does not matter
A Wien-bridge oscillator with gentle amplitude limiting, prized for low distortion
A crystal oscillator, which is the only way to make 1 kHz
Any square-wave generator, since shape is irrelevant
items.explanation: The Wien-bridge, with a soft limiter, is the classic low-distortion audio sine source, ideal here. The phase-shift type is more distorted, crystals are aimed at very high precision (and are awkward at audio), and a square wave is the wrong shape for a clean sine tone.

lessons.title: Relaxation & 555 Square/Triangle Generators
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Not every oscillator makes a smooth sine wave. Many useful signals are **square** (on/off) or **triangle** waves: the blink of an LED, the beep of a buzzer, the tick of a simple timer. These come from a different, very intuitive kind of oscillator called a **relaxation oscillator**.

**Build.** Forget Barkhausen for a moment; relaxation oscillators work by a charge-and-dump cycle, like a toilet tank or a dripping bucket that empties when it gets full. Here is the idea:
1. A **capacitor** (a part that stores charge) slowly fills up through a **resistor**. The voltage on it ramps upward.
2. A **comparator** (a circuit that watches a voltage and flips its output when a threshold is crossed) is watching. When the capacitor reaches an **upper threshold**, the comparator flips.
3. The flip causes the capacitor to discharge (dump its charge) down to a **lower threshold**, then the cycle repeats.

Watching the comparator's output, you see a **square wave** (it is either high or low). Watching the capacitor's voltage, you see a **triangle-ish wave** (ramping up, ramping down). The use of two thresholds, an upper and a lower, is called **hysteresis**, and it is what keeps the circuit cleanly flipping back and forth instead of chattering.

The speed depends on how fast the capacitor charges, which is set by R and C: bigger R or C means slower charging and a lower frequency. The period is roughly proportional to $R\\times C$.

**The 555 timer** is the world's most popular chip for this. Wired as an **astable** (free-running) oscillator, its frequency is about
$$f \\approx \\frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)\\,C}.$$
The capacitor charges through $R_A + R_B$ but discharges only through $R_B$, so the "high" time is longer than the "low" time. That uneven split is called the **duty cycle**, and for a basic 555 it is always more than 50 percent high.

**Recap.** A relaxation oscillator charges a capacitor to an upper threshold, dumps it to a lower one, and repeats, producing square and triangle waves. The 555 timer is the classic chip; R and C set the speed, and its high time naturally exceeds its low time.

items.prompt: Study the capacitor charge/discharge diagram. How does a relaxation oscillator make its repeating signal?
choices: By a resonant feedback loop producing a pure sine
By charging a capacitor up to a threshold, dumping it to a lower threshold, and repeating
By dividing the supply voltage with resistors
By converting AC to DC
items.explanation: A relaxation oscillator repeatedly charges a capacitor to an upper threshold, then discharges it to a lower one, like a bucket filling and dumping. That cycling makes the repeating wave. A resonant feedback loop making a sine is the sinusoidal (Barkhausen) type, not relaxation.

items.prompt: What kind of waveforms does a relaxation oscillator naturally produce?
choices: Only pure sine waves
Square waves (at the comparator output) and triangle-like waves (on the capacitor)
Only flat DC
Random noise
items.explanation: The comparator output flips between high and low, making a square wave, while the capacitor voltage ramps up and down, making a triangle-like wave. Relaxation oscillators are not the way to get a clean sine; that is what RC and LC sinusoidal oscillators are for.

items.prompt: What is the job of the comparator in a relaxation oscillator?
choices: To amplify the sine wave
To watch the capacitor voltage and flip its output when a threshold is crossed
To store charge
To set the supply voltage
items.explanation: A comparator watches a voltage and snaps its output when that voltage crosses a set threshold; here it flips when the capacitor hits the upper or lower trip point, driving the cycle. Storing charge is the capacitor's job, not the comparator's.

items.prompt: Using two trip points, an upper and a lower threshold, is called hysteresis. Why is it useful here?
choices: It makes the wave a perfect sine
It keeps the circuit cleanly flipping back and forth instead of chattering near one level
It removes the need for a capacitor
It sets the supply voltage
items.explanation: With two separate thresholds, the circuit must travel a clear gap between flips, giving a clean back-and-forth instead of jittery chatter around a single level. Hysteresis does not create a sine wave or eliminate the timing capacitor.

items.prompt: In a relaxation oscillator, if you increase the resistor R or the capacitor C, the frequency:
choices: Increases, because charging is faster
Decreases, because charging is slower (period is roughly proportional to R times C)
Stays exactly the same
Becomes negative
items.explanation: Bigger R or C means the capacitor charges more slowly, so each cycle takes longer and the frequency drops. Period grows roughly with R times C. The other options misread that relationship; frequency cannot be negative.

items.prompt: The 555 timer wired as an astable produces $f \\approx 1.44/((R_A+2R_B)C)$. With $R_A = 1\\,\\text{k}\\Omega$, $R_B = 1\\,\\text{k}\\Omega$, $C = 100\\,\\text{nF}$, the frequency is about:
choices: About 480 Hz
About 4.8 kHz
About 48 kHz
About 480 kHz
items.explanation: Here (RA + 2RB)C = (1000 + 2000) x 100e-9 = 3e-4, and 1.44/3e-4 is about 4800 Hz, or 4.8 kHz. The other choices are off by factors of ten.

items.prompt: In a basic 555 astable, why is the output high for longer than it is low (duty cycle above 50 percent)?
choices: The supply voltage is too high
The capacitor charges through $R_A + R_B$ but discharges only through $R_B$
The chip is defective
The comparator thresholds are random
items.explanation: Charging passes through both RA and RB (a longer path, so a longer high time), while discharging goes only through RB (shorter, so a shorter low time). That asymmetry pushes the duty cycle above 50 percent by design, not by defect.

items.prompt: How does a relaxation oscillator fundamentally differ from a sine-wave (Barkhausen) oscillator?
choices: It relies on a resonant 0-degree feedback loop to make a sine
It relies on charging a capacitor to thresholds and switching, not on a resonant in-step loop
It needs no resistor or capacitor
It only works above 1 GHz
items.explanation: A relaxation oscillator works by RC charging and comparator switching between thresholds, producing square/triangle waves, rather than by a resonant in-step feedback loop producing a sine. It very much uses an R and a C, and it is common at low frequencies, not just at gigahertz.

items.prompt: Which task is the 555 timer (as an astable) a natural fit for?
choices: Generating an ultra-pure sine wave for audio testing
Blinking an LED on and off at a chosen rate
Providing a temperature-stable frequency reference for a clock
Amplifying a tiny sensor voltage
items.explanation: A 555 astable produces a square wave whose rate you set with R and C, perfect for blinking an LED or driving a buzzer. It is not a clean sine source, not a high-precision reference (that is a crystal's job), and it is a timer, not an amplifier.

items.prompt: On the capacitor in a relaxation oscillator, what does the voltage waveform look like?
choices: A flat, unchanging line
A wave that ramps up to the upper threshold, then down to the lower threshold, repeatedly
A perfect sine wave
A single step that never returns
items.explanation: The capacitor voltage rises as it charges to the upper threshold, then falls as it discharges to the lower threshold, over and over, giving the triangle-like shape. It is neither flat nor a smooth sine, and it repeats rather than stepping once.

lessons.title: LC Oscillators: Colpitts & Hartley
lessons.content:
**Hook.** When you need a high-frequency signal, like the carrier of a radio transmitter at millions of cycles per second, RC oscillators struggle. The go-to building block becomes the **LC tank**: an **inductor (L)** and a **capacitor (C)** connected together, which naturally swap energy back and forth at one frequency, like a child on a swing.

**Build.** An inductor stores energy in a magnetic field; a capacitor stores it in an electric field. Wire them together and energy sloshes from one to the other and back, over and over, all on its own, this is **resonance**. The frequency it prefers is
$$f_0 = \\frac{1}{2\\pi\\sqrt{LC}}.$$
Like a swing pushed at just its natural rhythm, an LC tank is very *selective*: it strongly favors one frequency. We say it has a high **quality factor (Q)**, a measure of how sharply it picks out that one frequency. Higher Q gives a cleaner, steadier sine wave than an RC network can. That is why LC oscillators rule at radio frequencies.

The tank just needs a small nudge each cycle to replace lost energy, supplied by an amplifier through feedback. Two classic ways to tap the feedback off the tank give the two famous designs:
- **Colpitts oscillator:** the feedback is tapped from a **capacitor divider**, two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ in a row across the tank. The frequency uses their combined value $C_{eq} = \\dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ (the series combination).
- **Hartley oscillator:** the feedback is tapped from a **tapped inductor** ($L_1$, $L_2$) instead.

An easy memory hook: **C**olpitts uses **C**apacitors; Hartley uses the other one (inductors). A close cousin, the **Clapp**, adds one extra small capacitor in series with the coil to make the frequency steadier.

**Recap.** An LC tank (inductor plus capacitor) resonates at $f_0 = 1/(2\\pi\\sqrt{LC})$ with high Q, giving clean high-frequency sine waves. Colpitts taps feedback from a capacitor divider; Hartley taps it from a tapped inductor.

items.prompt: Study the Colpitts LC-tank diagram. What is an LC tank, and what does it do on its own?
choices: Two resistors that divide a voltage
An inductor and capacitor that swap energy back and forth, resonating at one frequency
A battery that stores charge
A switch that toggles on and off
items.explanation: An LC tank is an inductor (magnetic energy) plus a capacitor (electric energy); energy sloshes between them at a natural resonant frequency, like a swing. That natural oscillation is what the circuit exploits. It is not a resistor divider, a battery, or a switch.

items.prompt: The resonant frequency of an LC tank is:
choices: $f_0 = 2\\pi\\sqrt{LC}$
$f_0 = \\dfrac{1}{2\\pi\\sqrt{LC}}$
$f_0 = \\dfrac{1}{2\\pi RC}$
$f_0 = L \\times C$
items.explanation: The LC resonant frequency is f0 = 1/(2-pi-sqrt(LC)): larger L or C lowers the frequency. The first option inverts it, the 1/(2-pi-RC) form is for RC circuits (no inductor), and L times C is not a frequency at all.

items.prompt: Why are LC oscillators preferred over RC oscillators at radio frequencies?
choices: LC tanks are cheaper than resistors
An LC tank's high Q makes it very selective, giving a cleaner, steadier sine
RC networks cannot produce any phase shift
Inductors remove the need for an amplifier
items.explanation: The LC tank has a high quality factor (Q), so it sharply favors one frequency, yielding a purer and more stable sine than an RC network at high frequencies. RC networks do shift phase, an amplifier is still needed to replace lost energy, and cost is not the reason.

items.prompt: What does the quality factor (Q) of an LC tank tell you?
choices: How much DC voltage it can store
How sharply it picks out one frequency (its selectivity)
Its physical size
How many capacitors it contains
items.explanation: Q measures how sharply the tank favors its resonant frequency: high Q means a narrow, selective response and a cleaner, steadier oscillation. It is not a measure of stored DC, physical size, or part count.

items.prompt: What distinguishes a Colpitts oscillator from a Hartley oscillator?
choices: Colpitts taps feedback from a capacitor divider; Hartley taps from a tapped inductor
Colpitts uses no capacitor; Hartley uses no inductor
Colpitts works only at DC; Hartley only at audio
They are identical circuits
items.explanation: Both use an LC tank, but Colpitts takes its feedback from a divider of two capacitors, while Hartley takes it from a tapped inductor. Memory hook: Colpitts = Capacitors. Both still contain an inductor and a capacitor, and both are used at radio frequencies.

items.prompt: In a Colpitts oscillator with capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$, the frequency uses the combined capacitance:
choices: $C_{eq} = C_1 + C_2$ (simple sum)
$C_{eq} = \\dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ (series combination)
$C_{eq} = C_1 \\times C_2$
items.explanation: The two divider capacitors sit in series across the tank, so they combine as Ceq = (C1 x C2)/(C1 + C2). Adding them would be the parallel rule, and the product or difference are not how capacitances combine.

items.prompt: A Colpitts oscillator has $L = 1\\,\\mu\\text{H}$, $C_1 = 100\\,\\text{pF}$, $C_2 = 100\\,\\text{pF}$. First find $C_{eq}$, then estimate $f_0$.
choices: $C_{eq} = 200$ pF, $f_0 \\approx 11$ MHz
$C_{eq} = 50$ pF, $f_0 \\approx 22.5$ MHz
$C_{eq} = 50$ pF, $f_0 \\approx 1$ kHz
$C_{eq} = 100$ pF, $f_0 \\approx 16$ MHz
items.explanation: Two equal 100 pF capacitors in series give Ceq = (100 x 100)/(100 + 100) = 50 pF. Then f0 = 1/(2-pi-sqrt(1e-6 x 50e-12)) is about 22.5 MHz. Adding the capacitors (200 pF) would be the wrong rule, and the kilohertz answer is off by a huge factor.

items.prompt: Even a high-Q LC tank gradually loses energy. What keeps the oscillation going?
choices: Nothing; a real tank oscillates forever on its own
An amplifier feeds a small nudge of energy back into the tank each cycle
The capacitor generates new energy
The resistor adds energy
items.explanation: A real tank has losses, so its swinging would slowly die out. The amplifier, through feedback, tops up a little energy each cycle to keep the amplitude steady. A capacitor and resistor cannot create energy; resistors only dissipate it.

items.prompt: What does the Clapp oscillator add to a Colpitts, and why?
choices: A second amplifier, to boost output power
A small capacitor in series with the inductor, to make the frequency steadier
A resistor across the tank, to lower Q
A battery, to raise the supply voltage
items.explanation: The Clapp inserts a small extra capacitor in series with the coil so that capacitor mostly sets the frequency, swamping out drift from the transistor and improving stability. It is not about adding power, lowering Q, or changing the supply.

items.prompt: Why is the swing-on-a-playground analogy a good fit for an LC tank?
choices: A swing moves in a straight line, like current in a resistor
Energy sloshes back and forth at one natural rhythm, and a small push each cycle keeps it going
A swing never loses energy, just like an ideal capacitor
A swing only moves when pushed continuously
items.explanation: A swing has a natural rhythm and trades potential and kinetic energy back and forth, needing only a small timed push to replace losses, exactly like an LC tank trading magnetic and electric energy with a small nudge from the amplifier. A real swing does lose energy to friction, which is why the timed push matters.

lessons.title: Crystals, VCOs, PLLs & Phase Noise
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Your watch keeps time to within seconds a month, and your computer's clock ticks at a rock-steady billion-plus times a second. The unsung hero is a tiny sliver of **quartz crystal**. This lesson covers why crystals are so accurate, plus how we make an oscillator whose frequency we can *tune* and *lock*.

**Build: the crystal.** Quartz is **piezoelectric**: squeeze it and it makes a small voltage; apply a voltage and it physically flexes. A quartz sliver has an incredibly precise mechanical vibration, like a perfectly made tuning fork, and electrically it behaves like an LC tank with an *enormous* Q, thousands of times higher than any coil-and-capacitor tank. That super-high Q means the oscillator will only run at one extremely well-defined frequency that barely budges with temperature, supply voltage, or age. That steadiness is exactly what a **clock** needs, which is why crystals are the timing reference in watches, computers, and radios. For even tighter accuracy, a **TCXO** adds temperature compensation and an **OCXO** keeps the crystal in a tiny heated oven so its temperature never changes.

**Build: the VCO.** Sometimes you want to *change* the frequency with a control voltage. A **voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO)** does this, usually by including a **varactor**, a special diode whose capacitance changes with the voltage across it. Change the control voltage, change the capacitance, change the frequency.

**Build: the PLL.** A **phase-locked loop (PLL)** combines these ideas to make a stable, programmable frequency. It compares a VCO's output against a rock-steady crystal **reference** using a **phase detector**, smooths the comparison with a **loop filter**, and uses the result to steer the VCO until it locks. If the feedback divides the VCO frequency by a number $N$, then once locked:
$$f_{out} = N \\times f_{ref}.$$
So one crystal can generate a whole menu of precise, selectable frequencies, the heart of every radio tuner and computer clock chip.

**Build: phase noise.** No oscillator is perfect; its frequency jitters a tiny bit moment to moment. This is **phase noise**, and a cleaner oscillator has less of it. It matters most in radio and radar, where a noisy local oscillator can smear strong signals over weak ones and limit how well faint targets are seen. A PLL is clever here: close to the carrier it inherits the clean crystal reference's low noise, while farther out the VCO's own behavior dominates.

**Recap.** A crystal is a super-high-Q quartz resonator giving rock-steady frequency for clocks; a VCO tunes its frequency with a control voltage (often via a varactor); a PLL locks a VCO to a crystal reference so $f_{out} = N \\times f_{ref}$; phase noise measures an oscillator's tiny frequency jitter, critical in radio and radar.

items.prompt: Study the PLL block diagram. Why do quartz crystals give far more stable frequencies than ordinary LC tanks?
choices: Quartz is cheaper than a coil
A quartz crystal behaves like an LC tank with an enormous Q, so it favors one frequency extremely sharply
Quartz generates its own power
Crystals work only at very low frequencies
items.explanation: A quartz crystal's mechanical resonance acts like an LC tank with a Q thousands of times higher than a coil-and-capacitor tank, so it locks onto one very well-defined frequency that hardly drifts. The reason is the high Q, not cost, and crystals do not generate power.

items.prompt: Quartz is "piezoelectric." What does that mean?
choices: It conducts electricity like a metal
Squeezing it makes a voltage, and applying a voltage makes it physically flex
It glows when current passes through
It stores charge like a battery
items.explanation: Piezoelectric means mechanical stress and electrical voltage are linked: pressure produces a voltage, and a voltage produces a tiny physical movement. This coupling lets a quartz sliver's precise mechanical vibration act as an electrical resonator. It is not a metal conductor, a light source, or a battery.

items.prompt: Because a crystal's frequency barely shifts with temperature, supply, or age, crystals are the natural choice for:
choices: High-power amplifiers
Accurate timekeeping and clock references in watches, computers, and radios
Storing large amounts of energy
Smoothing out power-supply ripple
items.explanation: Rock-steady frequency is exactly what a clock needs, so crystals serve as the timing reference in watches, computers, and radios. They are not power devices, energy stores, or filter capacitors.

items.prompt: What do TCXO and OCXO add to a plain crystal oscillator?
choices: They make it tunable over a wide range
They fight temperature drift, by compensation (TCXO) or by holding the crystal in a heated oven (OCXO)
They convert its output to DC
They lower its Q for a broader response
items.explanation: A TCXO adds temperature compensation and an OCXO keeps the crystal at a fixed oven temperature, both to push frequency drift even lower. They improve stability, not tunability, and they do not rectify the output or deliberately lower Q.

items.prompt: What is a voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO)?
choices: An oscillator whose frequency is set by a control voltage
An oscillator that only works on AC power
An amplifier with no feedback
A crystal that cannot change frequency
items.explanation: A VCO's output frequency depends on an applied control voltage, so you can steer it electrically. That tunability is the opposite of a fixed crystal, and a VCO is an oscillator, not a feedback-free amplifier.

items.prompt: How does a varactor let a control voltage tune a VCO's frequency?
choices: It is a resistor whose value sets the gain
It is a diode whose capacitance changes with the voltage across it, shifting the tank frequency
It is a battery that powers the oscillator
It converts the sine wave to a square wave
items.explanation: A varactor is a diode whose junction capacitance varies with the applied voltage; placed in the resonant tank, changing its voltage changes the capacitance and therefore the frequency. It is not a gain-setting resistor, a power source, or a waveform shaper.

items.prompt: Name the three core blocks of a phase-locked loop (PLL) and their jobs.
choices: Resistor, capacitor, inductor: they store energy
Phase detector (compares), loop filter (smooths), VCO (the tunable oscillator being steered)
Two amplifiers and a battery
Rectifier, transformer, fuse
items.explanation: A PLL compares the VCO against a reference with a phase detector, smooths that error with a loop filter, and feeds the result to a VCO that gets steered until it locks. The other lists describe passive parts or unrelated power components.

items.prompt: A locked PLL divides the VCO frequency by $N$ before comparing it with the reference. The output frequency is then:
choices: $f_{out} = f_{ref} / N$
$f_{out} = N \\times f_{ref}$
$f_{out} = f_{ref} - N$
$f_{out} = f_{ref}$ regardless of N
items.explanation: Locking forces the divided VCO frequency to match the reference, so the VCO itself must run at N times the reference: f_out = N x f_ref. By choosing N you select from a menu of precise frequencies built from one crystal reference. Dividing or subtracting does not describe the locked relationship.

items.prompt: What is oscillator "phase noise"?
choices: The DC offset at the output
Tiny random jitter in the oscillator's frequency moment to moment
The loudness of the output tone
A type of crystal defect that stops oscillation
items.explanation: Phase noise is the small, random wobble in an oscillator's frequency or timing from one moment to the next; a cleaner oscillator has less of it. It is not a DC offset, a loudness measure, or a defect that halts oscillation.

items.prompt: Why does phase noise matter so much in radio and radar receivers?
choices: It increases the transmitter's power
A noisy local oscillator can smear strong signals over weak ones, hiding faint targets
It makes the antenna larger
It has no real effect on performance
items.explanation: In a receiver, local-oscillator phase noise spreads energy around the carrier so a strong nearby signal can mask a weak one, limiting the ability to detect faint targets. It does not boost transmit power or change antenna size, and it definitely does affect performance.

items.prompt: How does a PLL combine the strengths of its crystal reference and its VCO regarding phase noise?
choices: The VCO sets the noise everywhere
Close to the carrier it follows the clean crystal reference; farther out the VCO's own noise dominates
The reference sets the noise everywhere
A PLL removes phase noise entirely
items.explanation: Within the loop's reach the PLL forces the VCO to track the low-noise crystal reference (clean close-in), while beyond that reach the loop cannot correct fast enough and the VCO's own noise takes over. No real PLL removes phase noise completely.

items.prompt: A designer needs many precise, selectable frequencies but has only one accurate crystal. The standard solution is:
choices: Use a separate crystal for every frequency
Use a PLL to lock a VCO to the crystal reference and pick frequencies by choosing the divider N
Use a 555 timer for each frequency
Use an RC phase-shift oscillator and adjust the supply voltage
items.explanation: A PLL multiplies one crystal reference up to f_out = N x f_ref, so changing N selects from many precise frequencies, exactly how radios and clock chips work. Stocking a crystal per frequency is impractical, and 555 or RC oscillators lack the needed accuracy and stability.
